original_abstract: Wavelength-division multiplexing (WDM) has significantly advanced the complexity and functionality of optical neural networks (ONNs) by enabling parallel convolutional operations. However, transitioning these sophisticated architectures into practical, high-level integrated chips remains a significant obstacle. In this study, we demonstrate a microcomb-driven photonic processing unit (PPU) that implements convolution using a time-wavelength plane stretching technique. To optimize the performance of this chip-based processor, we developed a specialized control and operational protocol that achieves a record-breaking weight precision of 9 bits. The unit’s compact design and high-speed data loading capabilities result in an exceptional compute density exceeding 1 trillion operations per second per square millimeter (TOPS mm⁻²). We validated the system through two proof-of-concept experiments—image edge detection and handwritten digit recognition—achieving processing accuracy and capabilities comparable to traditional digital computers. Given its superior performance and inherent scalability, this parallel PPU offers a promising path toward revolutionizing hardware for intensive artificial intelligence tasks, such as autonomous driving, real-time video analysis, and advanced image reconstruction.
humanized_abstract: Recent optical neural networks (ONNs) rely on wavelength-division multiplexing (WDM) technology to achieve parallel convolution. Although they show great potential, moving these advanced architectures to high-level integrated chips is still a major challenge. Here, we present a microcomb-driven photonic processing unit (PPU) based on stretching time and wavelength in a time-wavelength plane to achieve convolution. In order to achieve the optimal performance of the photonic chip processor, a corresponding control protocol and working strategy was designed to achieve a weight precision of 9 bits, a value record in the field of photonic processing units. Finally, due to its compact structure and high data loading speed, the unit has a high compute density of over 1 trillion operations per second per square millimeter (TOPS mm−2). Experimental demonstrations including image edge detection and handwritten digit recognition, which are similar to those performed on a digital computer, show that the parallel PPU has great prospects for future artificial intelligence hardware such as autonomous driving, real-time video processing, and image super-resolution processing.
